knowledgeBase.summary: This knowledge base provides a comprehensive overview of VoIP and SIP telephony, covering essential concepts ranging from fundamental signaling protocols and media transport to advanced technical configurations. It serves as a practical guide for managing SIP trunking, optimizing codecs, resolving NAT traversal challenges, and troubleshooting call quality issues to ensure reliable voice communications.

sections.title: VoIP Fundamentals
sections.content:
### VoIP Fundamentals

VoIP architecture relies on the encapsulation of voice traffic into IP packets, primarily utilizing the **Real-time Transport Protocol (RTP)** for media and **Session Initiation Protocol (SIP)** for signaling.

#### Signaling and Media Flow
*   **SIP (RFC 3261):** Operates at the Application Layer, typically over UDP/5060 or TCP/TLS 5061. It manages session setup, modification, and teardown.
*   **RTP/RTCP:** Carries the actual audio stream. RTP uses dynamic UDP ports (typically 10000–20000 range). RTCP provides out-of-band statistics (jitter, packet loss).
*   **SDP (Session Description Protocol):** Embedded within SIP messages to negotiate codecs, IP addresses, and port numbers for the RTP stream.

#### Codec Selection and Bandwidth
Bandwidth calculation must account for Layer 2/3 overhead (IP/UDP/RTP headers = 40 bytes).
*   **G.711 (μ-law/a-law):** Uncompressed, 64 kbps payload. Total bandwidth per call: ~87 kbps.
*   **G.729:** Compressed, 8 kbps payload. Total bandwidth per call: ~31 kbps.

#### Quality of Service (QoS)
VoIP is latency-sensitive. Implement **Differentiated Services Code Point (DSCP)** tagging at the ingress switch port:
*   **Voice Bearer (RTP):** DSCP 46 (Expedited Forwarding - EF).
*   **Voice Signaling (SIP):** DSCP 24 (CS3).

**Cisco IOS Configuration Example:**
```bash
class-map match-any VOICE-TRAFFIC
 match dscp ef
 match dscp cs3

policy-map QOS-POLICY
 class VOICE-TRAFFIC
  priority percent 30

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 service-policy output QOS-POLICY
```

#### Troubleshooting Commands
Use these commands to verify connectivity and identify signaling bottlenecks.

**SIP Registration Verification (Asterisk CLI):**
```bash
sip show peers
# Check for "Status: OK" and correct latency (ms)
```

**Packet Capture (CLI/Linux):**
Capture signaling and media to diagnose one-way audio (typically NAT or firewall issues).
```bash
tcpdump -i eth0 udp portrange 5060-5061 or udp portrange 10000-20000 -w voip_trace.pcap
```

**Common Troubleshooting Matrix:**
| Symptom | Likely Cause |
| :--- | :--- |
| **One-way audio** | NAT/PAT blocking RTP stream; SIP ALG modifying headers incorrectly. |
| **Registration failure** | Incorrect credentials, DNS SRV record failure, or firewall blocking 5060. |
| **Choppy audio** | Jitter, insufficient bandwidth, or lack of QoS prioritization. |
| **Call drops at 30s** | SIP ACK not reaching the provider (NAT timeout or firewall). |

#### NAT Traversal
VoIP headers contain internal IP addresses. When traversing a NAT boundary, the SDP payload must be rewritten to the public IP. Use **STUN** (Session Traversal Utilities for NAT) or **ICE** (Interactive Connectivity Establishment) to resolve reachability. If using a Session Border Controller (SBC), ensure "SIP Header Manipulation" is configured to replace the `Contact` and `Via` headers with the public-facing interface address.

sections.title: SIP Protocol
sections.content:
### SIP Protocol

Session Initiation Protocol (SIP) is an application-layer control protocol (RFC 3261) used for establishing, modifying, and terminating multimedia sessions. It operates primarily over UDP/TCP port 5060 (unencrypted) or 5061 (TLS).

#### Transaction Model
SIP transactions consist of a request and its associated responses.
*   **Requests:** `INVITE`, `ACK`, `CANCEL`, `OPTIONS`, `BYE`, `REGISTER`, `INFO`, `SUBSCRIBE`, `NOTIFY`.
*   **Response Codes:**
    *   **1xx (Provisional):** 100 Trying, 180 Ringing, 183 Session Progress.
    *   **2xx (Success):** 200 OK.
    *   **3xx (Redirection):** 302 Moved Temporarily.
    *   **4xx (Client Error):** 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 486 Busy Here.
    *   **5xx (Server Error):** 500 Server Internal Error, 503 Service Unavailable.
    *   **6xx (Global Failure):** 603 Decline.

#### Message Structure
SIP messages are text-based, following an ABNF syntax similar to HTTP.
```text
INVITE sip:user@domain.com SIP/2.0
Via: SIP/2.0/UDP 192.168.1.5:5060;branch=z9hG4bK776asdz8
From: "Caller" <sip:100@domain.com>;tag=19283
To: <sip:200@domain.com>
Call-ID: a84b4c76e66710@192.168.1.5
CSeq: 314159 INVITE
Contact: <sip:100@192.168.1.5:5060>
Content-Type: application/sdp
Content-Length: 142
```

#### SDP Integration
The Session Description Protocol (SDP) is carried in the body of SIP messages to negotiate media parameters.
*   **m=audio:** Defines media type, port, and transport (e.g., `m=audio 16384 RTP/AVP 0 8 101`).
*   **a=rtpmap:** Maps payload types to codecs (e.g., `a=rtpmap:0 PCMU/8000`).
*   **c=IN IP4:** Defines the connection address for media streams.

#### Practical Diagnostics
For troubleshooting, use `sngrep` or `tcpdump` to capture traffic.

**Capture command:**
```bash
tcpdump -i any port 5060 -s 0 -w sip_capture.pcap
```

**Common Asterisk/FreePBX CLI Commands:**
*   `pjsip set logger on`: Enables SIP packet logging in the console.
*   `pjsip show endpoints`: Lists all configured SIP endpoints.
*   `pjsip show endpoint <name>`: Displays detailed configuration of a specific peer.
*   `pjsip show registrations`: Verifies status of inbound/outbound trunks.

#### Configuration Best Practices
*   **NAT Traversal:** Always define `external_media_address` and `external_signaling_address` in your SIP profile when behind a NAT/Firewall.
*   **Keep-Alives:** Use `qualify=yes` (Asterisk) or `keepalive` (Kamailio) to maintain NAT mappings in stateful firewalls.
*   **Security:** Implement `permit/deny` ACLs on trunks. Use `SIP Digest Authentication` for all endpoints. Disable `allowguest` to prevent unauthorized relaying.
*   **Transport:** Prefer `TLS` (SIPS) for signaling and `SRTP` for media to prevent eavesdropping and toll fraud via packet sniffing.

#### Header Precision
*   **Via:** Tracks the path taken by the request to ensure responses follow the same route.
*   **Record-Route:** Used by proxies to stay in the signaling path for the duration of the dialog.
*   **Contact:** Specifies the URI for direct communication, bypassing proxies for subsequent requests within the dialog.

sections.title: RTP & Media
sections.content:
### RTP & Media

Real-time Transport Protocol (RTP) carries voice and video payloads over UDP. In VoIP architectures, media flows are negotiated via SDP (Session Description Protocol) within SIP signaling.

#### RTP Header Structure
The 12-byte fixed header is critical for jitter buffer management and packet reordering:
*   **Payload Type (7 bits):** Defines the codec (e.g., PCMU=0, PCMA=8, G.729=18).
*   **Sequence Number (16 bits):** Increments by one for each RTP packet; used to detect packet loss and reorder out-of-order arrivals.
*   **Timestamp (32 bits):** Reflects the sampling instant of the first octet in the packet.
*   **SSRC (32 bits):** Synchronization Source identifier; uniquely identifies the media stream source.

#### Media Negotiation (SDP)
SDP defines the media capabilities. A typical `m=` line format:
`m=audio 16384 RTP/AVP 0 8 101`
*   `16384`: Port number for the media stream.
*   `RTP/AVP`: Profile (Audio/Video Profile).
*   `0 8 101`: Payload types (G.711u, G.711a, and RFC 2833/4733 DTMF).

#### Common Troubleshooting Commands

**1. Packet Capture (tcpdump)**
To isolate RTP traffic on a specific interface and port range:
```bash
tcpdump -ni eth0 udp portrange 10000-20000 -w media_capture.pcap
```
*Use `tshark -r media_capture.pcap -q -z rtp,streams` to analyze stream statistics and jitter.*

**2. Asterisk RTP Debugging**
To inspect RTP flow in real-time within the Asterisk CLI:
```bash
rtp set debug on
rtp set debug ip <remote_ip>
```
*Look for "Sent RTP packet" and "Got RTP packet" logs to verify bidirectional media flow.*

**3. RTCP Analysis**
RTCP (RTP Control Protocol) provides out-of-band statistics. Monitor these to identify network degradation:
*   **Jitter:** Variation in packet arrival time. High jitter (>30ms) causes audio artifacts.
*   **Fraction Lost:** Percentage of packets lost since the last report.
*   **RTT (Round Trip Time):** Calculated via RTCP Sender/Receiver Reports.

#### Configuration Best Practices
*   **Port Ranges:** Ensure firewalls are configured for the specific RTP port range defined in your PBX (e.g., `rtpstart=10000`, `rtpend=20000` in `rtp.conf`).
*   **DSCP Tagging:** To prioritize media over data, mark RTP packets with DSCP 46 (Expedited Forwarding).
    *   *Linux (iptables):* `iptables -t mangle -A OUTPUT -p udp --dport 10000:20000 -j DSCP --set-dscp 46`
*   **NAT Traversal:** If media is one-way, verify `nat=yes` or `force_rport=yes` in SIP configurations. Use STUN/TURN servers if the endpoint is behind a symmetric NAT.
*   **Codec Mismatch:** Ensure the `allow` list in your SIP peer configuration matches the SDP offer from the remote gateway. Use `transcoding` only when necessary, as it increases CPU load and latency.

#### Common Media Issues
*   **One-way Audio:** Usually caused by NAT/Firewall blocking the RTP port range or the SDP containing a private IP address (requires `externip` or `media_address` configuration).
*   **Clipping/Robotic Audio:** Indicates packet loss or excessive jitter. Check for interface congestion or duplex mismatches on the network switch.
*   **DTMF Failure:** Ensure `dtmfmode` (RFC2833, Inband, or Info) matches between endpoints. RFC 2833 is the industry standard.

sections.title: NAT Traversal
sections.content:
### NAT Traversal

Network Address Translation (NAT) disrupts real-time protocols (SIP/RTP) by modifying packet headers, causing signaling mismatches and one-way audio. Effective traversal requires managing the stateful mapping between private internal addresses and public WAN interfaces.

#### STUN (Session Traversal Utilities for NAT)
STUN (RFC 5389) allows an endpoint to discover its public IP and port mapping.
*   **Mechanism:** The client sends a Binding Request to a STUN server. The server returns the source IP/port seen on the public internet.
*   **Limitation:** Fails with Symmetric NAT, where the mapping changes based on the destination IP/port.

#### TURN (Traversal Using Relays around NAT)
When STUN fails (Symmetric NAT), TURN (RFC 5766) acts as a relay.
*   **Mechanism:** All media traffic is proxied through the TURN server.
*   **Configuration:** Requires high bandwidth allocation on the relay server.
*   **Syntax (Asterisk `rtp.conf`):**
    ```ini
    [general]
    stunaddr=stun.l.google.com:19302
    icesupport=yes
    ```

#### ICE (Interactive Connectivity Establishment)
ICE (RFC 8445) is the framework that orchestrates STUN and TURN. It gathers "candidates" (Host, Server Reflexive, Relayed) and performs connectivity checks to determine the optimal path.

#### SIP-Specific Handling
SIP headers (`Via`, `Contact`, `Record-Route`) often contain private IP addresses.
*   **SIP ALG (Application Layer Gateway):** Most SOHO routers implement SIP ALG, which attempts to rewrite SIP headers. **Recommendation:** Disable SIP ALG on all edge firewalls/routers, as it frequently corrupts SDP payloads.
*   **Manual NAT Configuration:** If the PBX is behind NAT, explicitly define the external address.
*   **Asterisk `sip.conf` (chan_sip):**
    ```ini
    [general]
    nat=force_rport,comedia
    externip=203.0.113.1
    localnet=192.168.1.0/255.255.255.0
    ```
*   **PJSIP Configuration:**
    ```ini
    [transport-udp]
    type=transport
    protocol=udp
    bind=0.0.0.0:5060
    external_media_address=203.0.113.1
    external_signaling_address=203.0.113.1
    ```

#### Port Forwarding vs. Port Triggering
*   **Port Forwarding:** Static mapping of WAN ports to internal PBX/Endpoint IPs. Use for static deployments.
    *   *Example (iptables):* `iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p udp --dport 5060 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.1.50:5060`
*   **Port Triggering:** Dynamic mapping based on outbound traffic. Generally discouraged for VoIP due to timeout risks and inconsistent port ranges.

#### Troubleshooting Commands
1.  **Verify Mapping:** Use `tcpdump` to inspect if the `Contact` header matches the public IP.
    `tcpdump -ni eth0 udp port 5060 -vv`
2.  **Check NAT Timeout:** If calls drop after 30 seconds, the NAT mapping has expired. Implement SIP Keep-Alives (Options packets) every 20-30 seconds.
    *   *Asterisk:* `qualify=yes` or `keep_alive=20`
3.  **Symmetric NAT Detection:** Use `pjnath-test` or similar utilities to identify if the router performs port-restricted mapping, which necessitates a TURN relay.

sections.title: Codecs
sections.content:
### Codecs

Codecs (coder-decoder) define the compression and decompression algorithms for digital audio/video streams. In VoIP, the choice of codec dictates the balance between bandwidth consumption, CPU overhead, and Mean Opinion Score (MOS).

#### Common Codec Specifications

| Codec | Bitrate (kbps) | Sampling (kHz) | Payload Size (ms) | Characteristics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **G.711 (PCMU/A)** | 64 | 8 | 20 | Uncompressed; high quality; high bandwidth. |
| **G.729** | 8 | 8 | 20 | CS-ACELP; high compression; high CPU load. |
| **G.722** | 64 | 16 | 20 | Wideband; superior clarity; requires G.722 support. |
| **Opus** | 6–510 | 8–48 | 20 | Adaptive; low latency; variable bitrate (VBR). |

#### Bandwidth Calculation
Bandwidth per call is calculated as: `(Codec Bitrate + IP/UDP/RTP Overhead) / Packetization Interval`.
*   **Overhead:** Standard RTP/UDP/IP header is 40 bytes (320 bits).
*   **Example (G.711 @ 20ms):** 
    *   Payload = 160 bytes (1280 bits).
    *   Total = 1600 bits per 20ms packet = 80 kbps per stream.

#### Asterisk Configuration (`codecs.conf` / `pjsip.conf`)
To prioritize codecs in Asterisk PJSIP, define the `disallow` and `allow` directives within the endpoint configuration. Order of `allow` statements defines preference.

```ini
[endpoint_template](!)
type=endpoint
disallow=all
allow=opus
allow=g722
allow=ulaw
```

#### Transcoding Management
Transcoding occurs when the ingress codec differs from the egress codec, consuming significant CPU cycles. Use `show translation` in the Asterisk CLI to monitor cost:

```bash
# Check translation paths and cost (in units of 1/256th of a second)
asterisk -rx "core show translation"
```

To minimize transcoding, ensure endpoints negotiate common codecs. If transcoding is unavoidable, monitor system load:

```bash
# Linux system load monitoring
top -b -n 1 | grep asterisk
# Check active media streams
asterisk -rx "pjsip show channels"
```

#### SDP Negotiation
Codecs are negotiated via Session Description Protocol (SDP) in the SIP INVITE/200 OK exchange. The `m=audio` line lists supported codecs in order of preference.

*   **Example SDP snippet:**
    `m=audio 16384 RTP/AVP 0 8 101`
    *   `0`: PCMU (G.711u)
    *   `8`: PCMA (G.711a)
    *   `101`: Telephone-event (RFC 2833/4733 DTMF)

#### Practical Implementation Notes
1.  **Packetization (ptime):** Standardize on 20ms. Lower values (10ms) increase packet rate/CPU load; higher values (30ms+) increase latency and jitter sensitivity.
2.  **DTMF Interop:** Always include `telephone-event` (RFC 4733) in the codec list. If missing, DTMF digits will fail to pass through gateways.
3.  **VAD/CNG:** Disable Voice Activity Detection (VAD) and Comfort Noise Generation (CNG) if experiencing "choppy" audio or one-way audio issues, as these can interfere with echo cancellers and jitter buffers.
4.  **Opus:** When using Opus, ensure `fmtp` parameters (e.g., `maxplaybackrate=48000`) are correctly passed in the SDP to avoid downsampling artifacts.

sections.title: SIP Trunking
sections.content:
### SIP Trunking

SIP Trunking replaces traditional TDM circuits (PRI/BRI) by delivering voice services over IP via the Session Initiation Protocol (RFC 3261). 

#### Core Architecture
*   **SIP Proxy/SBC:** Acts as the demarcation point. The Session Border Controller (SBC) handles NAT traversal, topology hiding, and protocol normalization.
*   **Transport:** Typically UDP/5060 for signaling; TCP/TLS (5061) for encrypted signaling. RTP/RTCP (typically UDP 10000–20000) handles media streams.
*   **Codec Negotiation:** SDP (Session Description Protocol) within the SIP INVITE/200 OK exchange determines the codec (G.711u/a, G.729, G.722).

#### Authentication Methods
1.  **IP-Based (Trusted):** The provider whitelists the enterprise public IP. No registration required.
2.  **Registration-Based:** The PBX sends a `REGISTER` request with credentials.
    *   *Syntax:* `REGISTER sip:provider.com SIP/2.0`
    *   *Header:* `Authorization: Digest username="user", realm="provider.com", nonce="...", uri="sip:provider.com", response="..."`

#### Configuration Example (Asterisk `pjsip.conf`)
For a standard IP-based trunk, define the endpoint, auth, and registration:

```ini
[my_trunk]
type=endpoint
context=from-trunk
disallow=all
allow=ulaw,alaw
outbound_auth=my_auth
aors=my_aor

[my_aor]
type=aor
contact=sip:provider_ip:5060

[my_auth]
type=auth
auth_type=userpass
username=trunk_user
password=secure_password
```

#### Troubleshooting & Diagnostics
Use `sngrep` or `tcpdump` to capture signaling. Filter by the provider’s IP to isolate the flow.

**Common CLI Commands:**
*   **Asterisk:** `pjsip set logger on` (Enable SIP debug)
*   **Cisco IOS:** `debug ccsip messages` (Display SIP signaling)
*   **Packet Capture:** `tcpdump -ni eth0 udp port 5060 -w trunk_trace.pcap`

#### Critical Parameters
*   **DTMF Relay:** Ensure alignment between PBX and provider. Use `rfc2833` (in-band signaling) or `info` (SIP INFO messages).
*   **NAT Traversal:** If the PBX is behind a NAT, ensure the `Contact` header reflects the public IP. Use `nat=force_rport,comedia` in Asterisk.
*   **Keep-Alives:** Use SIP `OPTIONS` packets to monitor trunk health.
    *   *Asterisk:* `qualify_frequency=60`
*   **Media Anchoring:** If one-way audio occurs, verify that the SDP `c=` (connection) line contains the correct public IP and that the RTP port range is open on the firewall.

#### Security Best Practices
1.  **ACLs:** Restrict inbound SIP traffic to the provider’s specific signaling IP ranges.
2.  **TLS/SRTP:** Enforce TLS for signaling and SRTP for media to prevent eavesdropping.
3.  **Rate Limiting:** Configure the SBC to drop INVITE floods to prevent toll fraud and resource exhaustion.
4.  **Header Sanitization:** Strip internal headers (e.g., `X-Asterisk-Info`) before forwarding to the provider to prevent topology leakage.

sections.title: DTMF
sections.content:
### DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency)

DTMF is the signaling system used for telephony to communicate dialed digits or control signals over an analog or digital voice channel. Each digit is represented by a unique combination of two sine waves (one low-frequency, one high-frequency).

#### Signaling Methods
In VoIP environments, DTMF transmission is categorized into three primary methods:

1.  **In-Band (Audio):** DTMF tones are sent as raw audio within the RTP stream. Highly susceptible to packet loss and compression artifacts (e.g., G.729 codec), which often causes digit corruption.
2.  **RFC 2833 / RFC 4733 (Out-of-Band):** DTMF tones are encapsulated as RTP events (payload type 101 by default). The audio stream remains clean, and the receiver reconstructs the tone based on the event packet. This is the industry standard for SIP-based systems.
3.  **SIP INFO:** DTMF digits are sent as SIP signaling messages (application/dtmf-relay). This occurs outside the media path.

#### Configuration Examples

**Asterisk (chan_sip)**
In `sip.conf`, define the DTMF mode globally or per-peer. `rfc2833` is the recommended setting.
```ini
[general]
dtmfmode=rfc2833

[peer_name]
dtmfmode=rfc2833
```

**FreeSWITCH**
In the dialplan or endpoint configuration, ensure the `dtmf_type` is set correctly.
```xml
<!-- In sip_profile/internal.xml -->
<param name="dtmf-type" value="rfc2833"/>
```

**Cisco IOS (Dial-Peer)**
To configure DTMF relay on a Cisco voice gateway, use the `dtmf-relay` command under the dial-peer:
```text
dial-peer voice 100 voip
 destination-pattern 555....
 session protocol sipv2
 dtmf-relay rfc2833
 codec g711ulaw
```

#### Troubleshooting & Diagnostics

*   **Packet Capture (Wireshark):** Filter by `rtp.p_type == 101` to verify RFC 2833 events. If you see the packets but the PBX does not respond, verify that the `telephone-event` payload type matches between endpoints.
*   **Codec Mismatch:** If using In-Band DTMF, ensure the codec is G.711 (u-law/a-law). Compressed codecs like G.729 or G.723.1 will almost certainly degrade the tone frequency, leading to "missed" digits.
*   **Gain Issues:** If digits are not detected, check for excessive jitter or low volume levels. DTMF detection requires a minimum signal-to-noise ratio; if the audio is too quiet, the DSP/software detector will fail.
*   **Payload Type Mismatch:** If one side expects RFC 2833 on payload 101 and the other sends it on 96, the digits will be ignored. Check the SDP `a=rtpmap` attribute in the SIP INVITE:
    `a=rtpmap:101 telephone-event/8000`

#### Best Practices
*   **Always prefer RFC 2833/4733.** It is the most robust method for modern VoIP networks.
*   **Disable In-Band DTMF** if RFC 2833 is active to prevent "double-dialing" or echo-related false positives.
*   **Interoperability:** When connecting to legacy PSTN gateways, ensure the gateway is configured to transcode or relay DTMF correctly, as some older TDM equipment only supports In-Band.

sections.title: Call Quality Troubleshooting
sections.content:
### Call Quality Troubleshooting

Effective troubleshooting requires isolating the fault domain: Layer 1/2 physical/link issues, Layer 3 QoS/routing, or SIP/RTP signaling anomalies.

#### 1. Network Path Analysis
Use `mtr` (My Traceroute) to identify jitter and packet loss at specific hops. Unlike `ping`, `mtr` provides continuous statistics per hop.
*   **Command:** `mtr -rw [destination_ip]`
*   **Thresholds:** Jitter > 30ms, Latency > 150ms (one-way), or Packet Loss > 1% typically result in audible degradation.

#### 2. QoS Verification
Ensure DSCP markings are preserved across the path. EF (Expedited Forwarding, 46) should be applied to RTP traffic, and AF41 to SIP signaling.
*   **Cisco IOS Verification:**
    ```bash
    show policy-map interface [interface_id]
    ```
    Look for "packet drops" in the priority queue. If drops exist, the bandwidth allocation for the `priority` command is insufficient.
*   **Packet Capture (PCAP):** Use `tcpdump` on the SBC or gateway to verify DSCP markings in the IP header.
    ```bash
    tcpdump -ni eth0 udp portrange 10000-20000 -v
    ```
    Check the `tos` field in the output.

#### 3. RTP Stream Analysis
If audio is one-way or choppy, analyze the RTP stream for sequence errors or out-of-order packets.
*   **Wireshark:** Navigate to **Telephony > RTP > RTP Streams**. Select the stream and click **Analyze**.
*   **Key Metrics:**
    *   **Delta:** Time between packets. High variance indicates jitter.
    *   **Jitter:** Variation in packet arrival time.
    *   **Skew:** Cumulative difference between expected and actual arrival.
    *   **Max Delta:** If this exceeds the jitter buffer size, audio clipping occurs.

#### 4. SIP Signaling & Codec Negotiation
Verify that the negotiated codec matches the bandwidth availability. G.711 (64kbps) is standard, but G.729 (8kbps) is preferred for bandwidth-constrained links.
*   **Check SDP (Session Description Protocol):**
    Ensure the `m=audio` line contains the correct port and codec payload types.
    ```sip
    m=audio 16384 RTP/AVP 0 8 101
    a=rtpmap:0 PCMU/8000
    a=rtpmap:101 telephone-event/8000
    ```
*   **Common Issue:** Mismatched DTMF methods (RFC 2833 vs. In-band). If DTMF fails, verify the `telephone-event` payload type in the SDP.

#### 5. Gateway/SBC Diagnostics
For VoIP gateways, check for DSP resource exhaustion.
*   **Cisco IOS:**
    ```bash
    show voice dsp group all
    show voice call summary
    ```
    If `State` shows `FXO/FXS` or `T1/E1` errors, check physical framing (e.g., `show controller t1`). Look for "Slip" errors, which indicate clock synchronization issues between the provider and the local PBX.

#### 6. Troubleshooting Checklist
1.  **Clocking:** If using T1/E1, ensure the local device is set to `clock source line`.
2.  **NAT/Firewall:** If one-way audio persists, verify that the SIP ALG is disabled on the edge firewall. SIP ALG often mangles the SDP `c=` (connection) line, causing the media stream to be sent to an unreachable private IP.
3.  **MTU:** If calls drop exactly at 30 seconds, check for MTU mismatches causing fragmented SIP packets that the firewall drops. Ensure MTU is set to 1500 or lower if tunneling (VPN/GRE) is involved.

glossary.definition: Session Initiation Protocol; a signaling protocol used to initiate, maintain, and terminate real-time communication sessions like voice and video.
Voice over Internet Protocol; a technology that enables voice communication and multimedia sessions over Internet Protocol networks.
Real-time Transport Protocol; a network protocol for delivering audio and video over IP networks, carrying the actual media stream.
Private Branch Exchange; a private telephone network used within an organization to manage internal and external calls.
Coder-Decoder; software or hardware that compresses and decompresses digital audio data to optimize bandwidth usage.
A method of delivering voice services and unified communications to customers equipped with IP-based PBX, replacing traditional analog lines.
The variation in the delay of received packets, which can cause audio distortion or choppy sound in VoIP calls.
The time it takes for a data packet to travel from the source to the destination; high latency results in noticeable delays during conversation.
Session Border Controller; a network element used to protect and regulate SIP-based voice traffic, often acting as a firewall for VoIP.
Direct Inward Dialing; a feature that allows callers to reach a specific extension on a PBX directly without going through an operator.
A software application that allows a computer or mobile device to function as a telephone for making VoIP calls.
The failure of one or more transmitted data packets to reach their destination, often resulting in audio gaps or robotic voice quality.

commonIssues.cause: NAT traversal issues blocking RTP traffic
commonIssues.solution: Configure STUN/TURN servers or adjust port forwarding on the firewall
Implement Quality of Service (QoS) tagging for voice traffic

commonIssues.cause: Incorrect credentials or firewall blocking SIP signaling (UDP 5060)
commonIssues.solution: Verify SIP account settings and ensure SIP ALG is disabled on the router

commonIssues.problem: Dropped calls after a specific interval
commonIssues.cause: SIP session timer mismatch or NAT timeout
commonIssues.solution: Adjust keep-alive intervals or session timer settings on the PBX

commonIssues.cause: Acoustic feedback or improper gain settings on hardware
commonIssues.solution: Enable echo cancellation on the VoIP gateway or adjust microphone sensitivity

commonIssues.cause: Public exposure of SIP port to malicious scanners
commonIssues.solution: Restrict SIP access to trusted IP ranges or use a VPN/SBC

commonIssues.cause: DNS resolution issues or inefficient routing
commonIssues.solution: Use local DNS caching or optimize the dial plan/routing table

commonIssues.cause: Incompatibility between provider and endpoint supported codecs
commonIssues.solution: Force a common codec like G.711 or G.729 on both ends

commonIssues.cause: Incorrect DID mapping or firewall blocking incoming INVITE requests
commonIssues.solution: Check DID configuration in the PBX and verify inbound firewall rules

commands.command:
class-map match-any VOICE-TRAFFIC
 match dscp ef
 match dscp cs3

policy-map QOS-POLICY
 class VOICE-TRAFFIC
  priority percent 30

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 service-policy output QOS-POLICY
commands.description: Cisco IOS configuration for QoS prioritization of voice traffic.

commands.command: tcpdump -i eth0 udp portrange 5060-5061 or udp portrange 10000-20000 -w voip_trace.pcap

commands.command: Authorization: Digest username="user", realm="provider.com", nonce="...", uri="sip:provider.com", response="..."
commands.description: SIP Authorization header syntax

commands.command:
[my_trunk]
type=endpoint
context=from-trunk
disallow=all
allow=ulaw,alaw
outbound_auth=my_auth
aors=my_aor

[my_aor]
type=aor
contact=sip:provider_ip:5060

[my_auth]
type=auth
auth_type=userpass
username=trunk_user
password=secure_password
commands.description: Asterisk pjsip.conf configuration snippet

a=rtpmap:0 PCMU/8000
